content:
# Luke Chapter 20 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 113-114 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_020_page_001.jpg, LUK_020_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass on one of the days, as he was teaching the people in the temple and proclaiming the good news, the chief priests and the scribes came upon him, together with the elders,

**2** and they spoke, saying to him, "Tell us by what authority you do these things, or who is the one who gave you this authority?"

**3** And having answered, he said to them, "I also will ask you a word, and tell me:

**4** The baptism of John — was it from heaven, or from men?"

**5** And they reasoned among themselves, saying, "If we should say, 'From heaven,' he will say, 'Why did you not believe him?'

**6** But if we should say, 'From men,' all the people will stone us, for they are persuaded that John was a prophet."

**7** And they answered that they did not know whence it was.

**8** And Jesus said to them, "Neither do I tell you by what authority I do these things."

**9** And he began to speak to the people this parable: "A man planted a vineyard and let it out to tenants, and went away for a considerable time.

**10** And at the season he sent to the tenants a servant, that they might give him of the fruit of the vineyard. But the tenants, having beaten him, sent him away empty.

**11** And he proceeded to send another servant. And having beaten that one also and having dishonored him, they sent him away empty.

**12** And he proceeded to send a third. And this one also they wounded and cast out.

**13** And the lord of the vineyard said, 'What shall I do? I will send my beloved son; perhaps they will respect him.'

**14** But having seen him, the tenants reasoned with one another, saying, 'This is the heir. Let us kill him, that the inheritance may become ours.'

**15** And having cast him out of the vineyard, they killed him. What therefore will the lord of the vineyard do to them?

**16** He will come and destroy these tenants and will give the vineyard to others." And having heard this, they said, "May it never be!"

**17** But having looked at them, he said, "What then is this that is written: 'The stone which the builders rejected — this has become the head of the corner'?

**18** Everyone who falls upon that stone shall be broken to pieces; but upon whomever it falls, it will crush him."

**19** And the scribes and the chief priests sought to lay hands on him in that very hour, and they feared the people; for they knew that he spoke this parable against them.

**20** And having watched closely, they sent spies who pretended themselves to be righteous, that they might seize upon his word, so as to hand him over to the rule and to the authority of the governor.

**21** And they questioned him, saying, "Teacher, we know that you speak and teach rightly, and you do not show partiality, but in truth you teach the way of God.

**22** Is it lawful for us to give tribute to Caesar, or not?"

**23** But having perceived their craftiness, he said to them,

**24** "Show me a denarius. Whose image and inscription does it have?" And they said, "Caesar's."

**25** And he said to them, "Then give back the things of Caesar to Caesar, and the things of God to God."

**26** And they were not able to seize upon his word before the people, and having marveled at his answer, they were silent.

**27** And some of the Sadducees, who deny that there is a resurrection, having come to him, questioned him,

**28** saying, "Teacher, Moses wrote for us that if a man's brother should die, having a wife, and this one should be childless, that his brother should take the wife and raise up seed for his brother.

**29** There were therefore seven brothers. And the first, having taken a wife, died childless.

**30** And the second,

**31** and the third took her; and likewise also the seven left no children and died.

**32** Afterward the woman also died.

**33** The woman therefore, in the resurrection, of which of them does she become wife? For the seven had her as wife."

**34** And Jesus said to them, "The sons of this age marry and are given in marriage;

**35** but those who are counted worthy to attain that age and the resurrection from the dead neither marry nor are given in marriage.

**36** For neither can they die anymore, for they are equal to angels and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection.

**37** But that the dead are raised, even Moses revealed at the bush, when he says, 'The Lord, the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob.'

**38** Now he is not God of the dead but of the living, for all live to him."

**39** And some of the scribes, having answered, said, "Teacher, you have spoken well."

**40** For they no longer dared to question him about anything.

**41** And he said to them, "How do they say that the Christ is the son of David?

**42** For David himself says in the book of Psalms, 'The Lord said to my Lord, "Sit at my right hand,

**43** until I set your enemies as a footstool of your feet."'

**44** David therefore calls him Lord; and how is he his son?"

**45** And in the hearing of all the people, he said to his disciples,

**46** "Beware of the scribes, who desire to walk about in long robes, and love greetings in the marketplaces and the first seats in the synagogues and the first places at the banquets,

**47** who devour the houses of the widows and for a pretense make long prayers. These shall receive greater condemnation."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 113-114) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 19:48 through Luke 20:24 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 19:48, Lk. 20:9, Lk. 20:17, and Lk. 20:24. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 20:34 through Luke 21:15 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 20:34, Lk. 20:46, Lk. 21:6, and Lk. 21:15. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΕΠΕΣΤΗΣΑΝ ΟΙ ΑΡΧΙΕΡΕΙΣ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΕΙΣ ΣΥΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΠΡΕΣΒΥΤΕΡΟΙΣ (20:1, "the chief priests and the scribes came upon him, together with the elders")
- ΕΝ ΠΟΙΑ ΕΞΟΥΣΙΑ ΤΑΥΤΑ ΠΟΙΕΙΣ (20:2, "by what authority you do these things")
- ΤΟ ΒΑΠΤΙΣΜΑ ΙΩΑΝΟΥ ΕΞ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΥ ΗΝ Η ΕΞ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ (20:4, "the baptism of John — was it from heaven, or from men")
- Ο ΛΑΟΣ ΑΠΑΣ ΚΑΤΑΛΙΘΑΣΕΙ ΗΜΑΣ (20:6, "all the people will stone us")
- ΠΕΠΕΙΣΜΕΝΟΣ (20:6, perfect passive — "persuaded/convinced" — settled conviction)
- ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΣ ΕΦΥΤΕΥΣΕΝ ΑΜΠΕΛΩΝΑ (20:9, "a man planted a vineyard")
- ΧΡΟΝΟΥΣ ΙΚΑΝΟΥΣ (20:9, "considerable times" — unique to Luke)
- ΤΟΝ ΥΙΟΝ ΜΟΥ ΤΟΝ ΑΓΑΠΗΤΟΝ (20:13, "my beloved son")
- ΟΥΤΟΣ ΕΣΤΙΝ Ο ΚΛΗΡΟΝΟΜΟΣ ΑΠΟΚΤΕΙΝΩΜΕΝ ΑΥΤΟΝ (20:14, "this is the heir; let us kill him")
- ΜΗ ΓΕΝΟΙΤΟ (20:16, "may it never be!" — strongest Greek negative wish)
- ΛΙΘΟΝ ΟΝ ΑΠΕΔΟΚΙΜΑΣΑΝ ΟΙ ΟΙΚΟΔΟΜΟΥΝΤΕΣ (20:17, Psalm 118:22 quotation)
- ΛΙΚΜΗΣΕΙ ΑΥΤΟΝ (20:18, "it will crush him" — winnowing/pulverization imagery)
- ΕΝΚΑΘΕΤΟΥΣ ΥΠΟΚΡΙΝΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ (20:20, "spies who pretended" — ΕΝΚΑΘΕΤΟΣ hapax in NT)
- ΚΑΙΣΑΡΙ ΦΟΡΟΝ ΔΟΥΝΑΙ (20:22, "to give tribute to Caesar")
- ΑΠΟΔΟΤΕ ΤΑ ΚΑΙΣΑΡΟΣ ΚΑΙΣΑΡΙ ΚΑΙ ΤΑ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΤΩ ΘΕΩ (20:25, "give back the things of Caesar to Caesar, and the things of God to God")
- ΤΩΝ ΣΑΔΔΟΥΚΑΙΩΝ ΟΙ ΑΝΤΙΛΕΓΟΝΤΕΣ ΑΝΑΣΤΑΣΙΝ ΜΗ ΕΙΝΑΙ (20:27, "the Sadducees, who deny that there is a resurrection")
- ΙΣΑΓΓΕΛΟΙ (20:36, "equal to angels" — hapax legomenon in NT)
- ΚΑΙ ΜΩΥΣΗΣ ΕΜΗΝΥΣΕΝ ΕΠΙ ΤΗΣ ΒΑΤΟΥ (20:37, "even Moses revealed at the bush")
- ΠΑΝΤΕΣ ΓΑΡ ΑΥΤΩ ΖΩΣΙΝ (20:38, "for all live to him")
- ΕΙΠΕΝ ΚΥΡΙΟΣ ΤΩ ΚΥΡΙΩ ΜΟΥ (20:42, Psalm 110:1 quotation)
- ΥΠΟΠΟΔΙΟΝ ΤΩΝ ΠΟΔΩΝ ΣΟΥ (20:43, "a footstool of your feet")
- ΠΡΟΣΕΧΕΤΕ ΑΠΟ ΤΩΝ ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΕΩΝ (20:46, "beware of the scribes")
- ΠΕΡΙΣΣΟΤΕΡΟΝ ΚΡΙΜΑ (20:47, "greater condemnation")

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **20:1 — "came upon" vs. "stood-upon [him]" vs. "came up to him":** EMET renders "stood-upon [him]," LOGOS "came upon him," PNEUMA "came up to him." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "came upon" renders ΕΠΕΣΤΗΣΑΝ with appropriate confrontational force without EMET's brackets; PNEUMA's "came up to" loses the aggressive nuance of the verb.

2. **20:1 — "together with" vs. "with" vs. "along with":** EMET renders "with," LOGOS "with," PNEUMA "along with." SOPHIA renders "together with" — this renders ΣΥΝ with slightly more precision than bare "with," distinguishing it from ΜΕΤΑ, while avoiding PNEUMA's informal "along with."

3. **20:1 — "proclaiming the good news" vs. "proclaiming-the-good-news" vs. "proclaiming the gospel":** EMET renders "proclaiming-the-good-news," LOGOS "proclaiming the gospel," PNEUMA "proclaiming the good news." SOPHIA follows PNEUMA — "proclaiming the good news" renders ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙΖΟΜΕΝΟΥ transparently without EMET's hyphens; LOGOS's "gospel" imports a technical English term where the Greek simply means "good news."

4. **20:6 — "are persuaded" vs. "having been persuaded" vs. "are convinced":** EMET renders "are having been persuaded," LOGOS "are persuaded," PNEUMA "are convinced." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "are persuaded" renders ΠΕΠΕΙΣΜΕΝΟΣ (perfect passive) as a present state resulting from past persuasion; EMET's "are having been persuaded" is over-literal; PNEUMA's "convinced" is acceptable but less precise.

5. **20:6 — "was a prophet" vs. "is a prophet":** EMET renders "is a prophet," LOGOS "was a prophet," PNEUMA "was a prophet." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "was" is appropriate in English indirect discourse reporting the people's belief about John, who by this point in the narrative has been executed.

6. **20:9 — "tenants" vs. "tenant-farmers":** EMET renders "tenant-farmers," LOGOS "tenants," PNEUMA "tenant farmers." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "tenants" renders ΓΕΩΡΓΟΙΣ concisely; the farming context is self-evident from "vineyard."

7. **20:13 — "perhaps they will respect him" vs. "perhaps they will respect this [one]":** EMET renders "perhaps they will respect this [one]," LOGOS "perhaps they will respect him," PNEUMA "perhaps they will respect him." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "him" is natural English; EMET's "this [one]" with brackets is unnecessarily literal.

8. **20:17 — "the head of the corner" vs. "the cornerstone":** EMET renders "head of [the] corner," LOGOS "the head of the corner," PNEUMA "the cornerstone." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the head of the corner" preserves ΚΕΦΑΛΗΝ ΓΩΝΙΑΣ faithfully; PNEUMA's "cornerstone" collapses the metaphor into a single English word that may obscure whether a foundation stone or capstone is meant.

9. **20:18 — "shall be broken to pieces" vs. "will be broken-to-pieces" vs. "will be broken to pieces":** EMET renders "will be broken-to-pieces," LOGOS "shall be broken," PNEUMA "will be broken to pieces." SOPHIA renders "shall be broken to pieces" — combining LOGOS's formal "shall" with the fuller rendering "broken to pieces" for ΣΥΝΘΛΑΣΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ (EMET and PNEUMA agree on "to pieces").

10. **20:18 — "it will crush him" vs. "it will scatter him like dust":** EMET renders "it will crush him," LOGOS "it will scatter him like dust," PNEUMA "it will crush anyone on whom it falls." SOPHIA follows EMET — "it will crush him" renders ΛΙΚΜΗΣΕΙ ΑΥΤΟΝ concisely; LOGOS's "scatter him like dust" is interpretive (adding "like dust"); PNEUMA restructures the clause.

11. **20:21 — "you do not show partiality" vs. "you do not receive [the] face [of anyone]" vs. "you do not receive a face":** EMET renders "you do not receive [the] face [of anyone]," LOGOS "do not receive a face," PNEUMA "show no favoritism." SOPHIA renders "you do not show partiality" — this renders the Semitism ΟΥ ΛΑΜΒΑΝΕΙΣ ΠΡΟΣΩΠΟΝ in clear English while preserving its meaning; EMET and LOGOS preserve the idiom but require brackets or produce obscure English; PNEUMA's "favoritism" is slightly colloquial.

12. **20:25 — "give back" vs. "render" vs. "give back to":** EMET renders "give-back," LOGOS "render," PNEUMA "give back to." SOPHIA renders "give back" — this renders ΑΠΟΔΟΤΕ with the crucial "back" element (ΑΠΟ-) that distinguishes it from simple giving; LOGOS's "render" is traditional but archaic; EMET hyphens unnecessarily.

13. **20:25 — "Then" vs. "Therefore":** EMET renders "Therefore," LOGOS "Then," PNEUMA "Then." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "Then" renders ΤΟΙΝΥΝ naturally; "therefore" is acceptable but slightly heavier than the particle warrants.

14. **20:27 — "who deny that there is a resurrection" vs. "those saying [there] is no resurrection":** EMET renders "those saying [there] is no resurrection," LOGOS "who deny that there is a resurrection," PNEUMA "who deny that there is a resurrection." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "who deny" renders ΑΝΤΙΛΕΓΟΝΤΕΣ ("speaking against") with appropriate force; EMET's literal "saying" misses the adversative prefix ΑΝΤΙ-.

15. **20:35 — "counted worthy" vs. "considered-worthy" vs. "considered worthy":** EMET renders "considered-worthy," LOGOS "counted worthy," PNEUMA "considered worthy." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "counted worthy" renders ΚΑΤΑΞΙΩΘΕΝΤΕΣ with formal English; EMET hyphenates; PNEUMA's "considered" is acceptable but "counted" better conveys the deliberate divine reckoning.

16. **20:37 — "revealed" vs. "disclosed" vs. "made this clear":** EMET renders "disclosed," LOGOS "revealed," PNEUMA "made this clear." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "revealed" renders ΕΜΗΝΥΣΕΝ with appropriate weight; EMET's "disclosed" is acceptable but slightly clinical; PNEUMA paraphrases.

17. **20:37 — "when he says" vs. "as he says" vs. "where he calls":** EMET renders "as he says," LOGOS "when he calls," PNEUMA "where he calls." SOPHIA renders "when he says" — combining LOGOS's temporal "when" with EMET's and the Greek's "says" (ΛΕΓΕΙ); PNEUMA's "where" treats the bush passage as a location marker, which is possible but less natural.

18. **20:38 — "Now he is" vs. "But he is":** EMET renders "But he is," LOGOS "Now he is," PNEUMA "He is." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Now" renders ΔΕ as a transitional particle rather than an adversative; the shift here is from argument to conclusion, not contrast.

19. **20:44 — "David therefore calls him Lord; and how is he his son?" vs. "David therefore calls him Lord, and how is he his son?":** All three translators agree on the substance. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's punctuation with semicolon, separating the statement from the question for clarity.

20. **20:45 — "in the hearing of all the people" vs. "[while] all the people [were] hearing":** EMET renders "[while] all the people [were] hearing," LOGOS "in the hearing of all the people," PNEUMA "while all the people were listening." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "in the hearing of all the people" renders ΑΚΟΥΟΝΤΟΣ ΠΑΝΤΟΣ ΤΟΥ ΛΑΟΥ with formal precision; EMET uses brackets; PNEUMA paraphrases.

21. **20:46 — "who desire to walk about in long robes" vs. "those wanting to walk in long-robes" vs. "they love to walk around in flowing robes":** EMET renders "those wanting to walk in long-robes," LOGOS "who desire to walk about in long robes," PNEUMA "they love to walk around in flowing robes." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "who desire" renders ΘΕΛΟΝΤΩΝ formally; "walk about" captures ΠΕΡΙΠΑΤΕΙΝ; "long robes" renders ΣΤΟΛΑΙΣ without EMET's hyphens; PNEUMA's "flowing robes" is interpretive.

22. **20:47 — "greater condemnation" vs. "more-abundant judgment" vs. "most severe punishment":** EMET renders "more-abundant judgment," LOGOS "greater condemnation," PNEUMA "most severe punishment." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "greater condemnation" renders ΠΕΡΙΣΣΟΤΕΡΟΝ ΚΡΙΜΑ precisely; EMET's "more-abundant" is over-literal and hyphenated; PNEUMA's superlative "most severe" exceeds the comparative, and "punishment" is interpretive for ΚΡΙΜΑ ("judgment/condemnation").

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
